title: Topological transitions in spin-ice induced by geometrical constraints
authors: R. A. Borzi, E. S. Loscar, S. A. Grigera
abstract: We study the nearest-neighbor spin-ice model subjected to a magnetic field applied along the global [111] and [110] directions, focusing on the role of sample geometry in stabilizing topological phase transitions. While no Kasteleyn transition is expected for this field orientations in the thermodynamic limit, we show that constraining the transverse dimensions of the system qualitatively changes the behavior. For samples elongated along the field direction with finite transverse area, the divergence-free constraint quantizes the number of string excitations that can span the system. As a result, the magnetization evolves through a cascade of discrete transitions corresponding to the successive entry of individual strings. Using Monte Carlo simulations, we demonstrate that each transition is marked by sharp magnetization steps and peaks in the specific heat and susceptibility, whose amplitudes scale linearly with the system length. We complement the numerical results with an analytical treatment based on the entropy - energy balance on a system with reduced dimensionality, deriving the critical fields associated with each topological sector. In the isotropic limit these transitions merge into a smooth crossover, but for anisotropic samples they remain sharply resolved, illustrating an unconventional mechanism by which finite geometry stabilizes topological phase transitions in frustrated magnets.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the shape of a sample can fundamentally change the nature of phase transitions in frustrated magnets. By restricting the transverse dimensions of a spin-ice system, the authors show that continuous crossovers are replaced by a cascade of discrete, topological transitions. These transitions are driven by the quantized entry of string-like excitations into the system. The study provides both numerical evidence via Monte Carlo simulations and an analytical framework to predict these transitions based on entropy-energy competition.
main_problem: Investigating how geometrical constraints and finite transverse dimensions can stabilize and induce discrete topological phase transitions in spin ice, which would otherwise be smooth crossovers in the thermodynamic limit.
main_result: The study shows that constraining the transverse dimensions of a spin-ice system quantizes the number of system-spanning string excitations, leading to a cascade of discrete magnetization steps and sharp peaks in specific heat and susceptibility.
method: The authors use Monte Carlo simulations to characterize the sequence of transitions and scaling behavior, complemented by an analytical treatment based on an entropy-energy balance in reduced dimensions.
model_or_system: A nearest-neighbor spin-ice model on a pyrochlore lattice subjected to magnetic fields applied along the [111] and [110] directions.
key_observables: Magnetization (M), specific heat (Cv), magnetic susceptibility (chi), monopole density, and the number of system-spanning strings.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (h), temperature (T), longitudinal dimension (L_parallel), and transverse dimension (L_perp).
main_assumptions: The analytical prediction for critical fields assumes a semi-infinite system and may break down for very small system sizes due to the influence of closed-loop excitations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the pyrochlore lattice and string excitations; Figure 2 shows magnetization steps and specific heat peaks for different geometries; Figure 6 compares smooth vs. jump-like behavior in different field directions; Figure 7 validates analytical predictions for critical fields.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of geometric stabilization of transitions, describes the spin-ice model and geometry, presents Monte Carlo results for magnetization and heat capacity, provides an analytical derivation for critical fields, and discusses scaling and the thermodynamic limit.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a- A conventional, cubic unit cell for the pyrochlore lattice, made of one set of tetrahedra pointing down (marked in gray) and another one pointing up. The two-in/two-out spin configuration is compatible with a saturated state both for B ∥[111] or B ∥[110]. For B ∥[111] the lattice can be thought as made of spins on triangular planes (dashed arrows) and spins on Kagome planes (dark triangles). For B ∥[110], it can be thought as composed of α−chains (lines of black spins, with a component in the direction of [110]) and β−chains (lines of red spins perpendicular to the field). The cell constants a, b, and c are indicated in magenta. They allow for periodic boundary conditions...

FIG. 2. Upper panel: Magnetization as a function of x = h/T for lattices of different geometries with B ∥[111] and T/Jeff = 0.3. The smooth black curve corresponds to the isotropic case, L × L × L with L = 8. The stepped curves were measured in samples elongated along the [111] axis with dimensions L∥× L⊥× L⊥, where L⊥= 4 and L∥= 8L⊥× n, with the value of n indicated in the legend. The case n = 1 contains the same number of spins as the isotropic system. Horizontal dashed lines mark the magnetization steps corre- sponding to the successive introduction of strings of negative magnetization that extend across the sample. Lower panel: Specific heat as a function of x = h/T. The height of the...

FIG. 3. Magnetization vs x = h/T simulated for a system with L⊥= 3, and L∥in the direction of B ∥[111]. The verti- cal lines indicate the critical xi deduced from the exhaustive counting method (see text), while the equidistant horizontal ones indicate multiples of the magnetization of a string of spins running along [111]. Surprisingly, there is a half jump around x = 0, with a diverging susceptibility in a symmetry point. Although this resembles a ferromagnetic critical point, the transition is first order but with no domains.

FIG. 4. Number of spin configurations Ω1 (in blue) with one string going through a fixed up tetrahedron and out through any down tetrahedron (painted gray). Along the text we take the fixed up tetrahedron as the (1, 1) one. Ω2(i, j) (in black) designates this number for two strings, one passing through the (1,1) and the other through one of the different up tetra- hedra at the location (i, j) ̸= (1, 1). For 0 strings, Ω0 = 9 for L⊥= 2, and Ω0 = 42 for L⊥= 3. Ωn was calculated through the exhaustion method up to lattices with 9 unit cells in the direction perpendicular to the [111] plane.

FIG. 5. A sample of 4 × 1 × 1 rombohedral unit cells used in our simulations, with non-perpendicular cell-vectors a, b, and c of equal modulus. We mark up (orange) and down (grey) tetrahedra, α−chains parallel to the field B ∥a ∥[110], and perpendicular β−chains. The real set of spins and tetrahedra are drawn in continuous line, but we include copies of them linked by PBC in different types of line to help visualization. There are inverted α−spins, although no string has entered the system: this PBC allow the insertion of spin loops (made of inverted α, in green, and β−spins, in dark red) contained into Kagome planes. These loops change de magnetization along [110], but do not change the...

FIG. 6. Magnetization (upper panel), specific heat C (middle panel), and number density of monopoles per tetrahedron ρ (lower panel) for samples of size L∥×L⊥×L⊥with B ∥[110], with the longest side of the sample oriented along the field. L⊥= 3 and L∥= 4L⊥× n, where n ranges from 1 to 9. The inset to the middle panel shows the increase in height of the peak in magnetic susceptibility χ as a function of L∥at x1, suggesting a first order transition. The same behavior is observed in C. Remarkably, ρ seems to copy some features from the response functions.
